data_flow_trace.original: Attacker controls GOVERNANCE role and calls GovernanceConfig.setForNextEpoch(0, 0, 1) at GovernanceConfig.sol:119 — _validateConfig only checks votingDurationMicros > 0; minVotingThreshold=0 and requiredProposerStake=0 pass
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: setForNextEpoch still requires GOVERNANCE and forwards to _validateConfig(_minVotingThreshold, _requiredProposerStake, _votingDurationMicros) (3-arg, not 1-arg) — any of the trivial values (0,0,1) revert before the pending config is written.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: Issue assumes _validateConfig signature is _validateConfig(uint64 _votingDurationMicros) with only votingDurationMicros==0 check. Current signature is _validateConfig(uint128, uint256, uint64) with 6 lower/upper bound checks.

data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: _validateConfig now enforces: _minVotingThreshold != 0 (revert InvalidVotingThreshold); != type(uint128).max; _requiredProposerStake != 0 (revert InvalidProposerStake); != type(uint256).max; _votingDurationMicros >= MIN_VOTING_DURATION (1 hour = 3_600_000_000 micros); <= MAX_VOTING_DURATION (365 days).
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: minVotingThreshold > 0 AND < type(uint128).max; requiredProposerStake > 0 AND < type(uint256).max; 3_600_000_000 <= votingDurationMicros <= 31_536_000_000_000_000
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: The entire premise of the finding — that _validateConfig only checks votingDurationMicros==0 — is false. All three trivial-value attacks (0, 0, 1) are independently rejected.

data_flow_trace.original: applyPendingConfig() writes (0, 0, 1) to live state at epoch boundary.
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: applyPendingConfig still copies _pendingConfig into live storage, but no such malicious pending config can ever exist because setForNextEpoch reverts on (0, 0, 1) at line 139 before assignment at lines 141-146.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: requireAllowed(RECONFIGURATION); hasPendingConfig == true (only set via setForNextEpoch, which validates inputs)
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: Step is unreachable with the malicious values; the pending-config pattern itself is unchanged but pre-validated.

data_flow_trace.original: createProposal passes because requiredStake=0 and votingPower never < 0.
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: createProposal still contains `if (votingPower < requiredStake) revert`, but requiredProposerStake is guaranteed > 0 by _validateConfig; thus the check is effective (requires nonzero votingPower for any valid config).
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: votingPower (from stake pool at expirationTime) >= requiredProposerStake > 0

data_flow_trace.original: Attacker votes in same block because expirationTime = now + 1µs; 1 wei yes-vote wins with minVotingThreshold=0.
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: votingDurationMicros is now >= 3_600_000_000 (1 hour), so expirationTime is at least one hour in the future — voting cannot resolve in the same block. Additionally, canResolve() at lines 217-220 enforces `now_ > lastVoteTime` (atomicity guard) preventing same-block vote+resolve flash-loan attacks. minVotingThreshold is guaranteed > 0, so the `yesVotes + noVotes >= minVoteThreshold` quorum check is non-trivial.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: now_ >= p.expirationTime (>= 1 hour after creation); now_ > lastVoteTime; yesVotes > noVotes && yesVotes + noVotes >= minVoteThreshold (>=1)
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: 1-microsecond voting window is impossible; same-block vote+resolve blocked by atomicity guard; zero-quorum impossible.

data_flow_trace.original: Executor calls execute() with arbitrary datas — governance captured.
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: Execute path is unreachable because the prerequisite (a SUCCEEDED proposal created under trivial thresholds) cannot exist.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: Proposal must reach SUCCEEDED state, which requires real quorum and waiting out a real voting window.

reasoning_chain: The finding's root cause is the claim that _validateConfig has only a single check `_votingDurationMicros == 0`. In the current code at src/runtime/GovernanceConfig.sol:188-213, _validateConfig is a 3-parameter function enforcing lower bounds on all three config values: minVotingThreshold must be in (0, type(uint128).max); requiredProposerStake must be in (0, type(uint256).max); votingDurationMicros must be in [MIN_VOTING_DURATION=1 hour in micros, MAX_VOTING_DURATION=365 days in micros]. The attack input (0, 0, 1) is rejected at the very first revert (InvalidVotingThreshold) and would also fail the other five checks. Additionally, Governance.sol:217-220 adds a flash-loan atomicity guard in canResolve() (`now_ > lastVoteTime`) that independently blocks same-block vote+resolve even if a short window existed. The vulnerability as described is fully mitigated.

key_evidence.excerpt: function _validateConfig(uint128 _minVotingThreshold, uint256 _requiredProposerStake, uint64 _votingDurationMicros) internal pure { if (_minVotingThreshold == 0) revert Errors.InvalidVotingThreshold(); if (_minVotingThreshold == type(uint128).max) revert Errors.ExcessiveMinVotingThreshold(); if (_requiredProposerStake == 0) revert Errors.InvalidProposerStake(); if (_requiredProposerStake == type(uint256).max) revert Errors.ExcessiveRequiredProposerStake(); if (_votingDurationMicros < MIN_VOTING_DURATION) revert Errors.VotingDurationTooShort(...); if (_votingDurationMicros > MAX_VOTING_DURATION) revert Errors.VotingDurationTooLong(...); }
key_evidence.why_matters: Directly refutes the premise of the finding. Every parameter in the malicious input (0, 0, 1) hits an independent revert.

key_evidence.excerpt: uint64 public constant MIN_VOTING_DURATION = uint64(1 hours) * 1_000_000; uint64 public constant MAX_VOTING_DURATION = uint64(365 days) * 1_000_000;
key_evidence.why_matters: Lower bound of 1 hour (3.6e9 micros) makes the '1 microsecond voting window' attack impossible.

key_evidence.excerpt: function setForNextEpoch(uint128 _minVotingThreshold, uint256 _requiredProposerStake, uint64 _votingDurationMicros) external { requireAllowed(SystemAddresses.GOVERNANCE); _requireInitialized(); _validateConfig(_minVotingThreshold, _requiredProposerStake, _votingDurationMicros); _pendingConfig = PendingConfig({...}); hasPendingConfig = true; ... }
key_evidence.why_matters: Confirms that validation runs before any pending config is written. No bypass path.

key_evidence.excerpt: function canResolve(...) { ... uint64 lastVote = lastVoteTime[proposalId]; if (lastVote > 0 && now_ <= lastVote) { return false; } return true; }
key_evidence.why_matters: Defense-in-depth: even if duration were somehow 1 micro, atomic same-block vote+resolve is blocked by lastVoteTime atomicity guard.

key_evidence.excerpt: if (p.yesVotes > p.noVotes && p.yesVotes + p.noVotes >= p.minVoteThreshold) return ProposalState.SUCCEEDED;
key_evidence.why_matters: Quorum check uses minVoteThreshold; guarantees >=1 because _validateConfig forbids minVotingThreshold==0.

counterargument: One could argue that despite the bounds, the minimum values (minVotingThreshold=1, requiredProposerStake=1, votingDurationMicros=3_600_000_000) are still economically trivial: a GOVERNANCE role holder could set requiredProposerStake=1 wei and minVotingThreshold=1 vote, then wait 1 hour and pass any proposal with essentially no collateral. This would still enable a cheap takeover IF the attacker already controls GOVERNANCE. However, (a) the issue's specific exploit (same-block 1-µs window, zero quorum) is impossible; (b) the root precondition is 'attacker controls GOVERNANCE' which means governance is already captured — lowering thresholds adds no new capability beyond what GOVERNANCE already has (GOVERNANCE can call any privileged system function directly via setForNextEpoch and other config contracts); (c) the 1-hour minimum voting window gives off-chain defenders time to observe and potentially counter-propose/emergency-pause. The finding as written claims a specific high-severity exploit chain (1-wei, same-block, zero-quorum takeover) which is categorically blocked.
